title: Constructions in symplectic and contact topology via h-principles
speaker: Oleg Lazarev
abstract: Certain `flexible' structures in symplectic and contact topology satisfy h-principles, meaning that their geometry reduces to underlying topological data. Although these flexible structures have no interesting geometry by themselves, I will show how h-principles provide a unified approach to various constructions in symplectic and contact topology and can be used to build new exotic structures that are geometrically interesting.

proof_stages.label: Weinstein handlebody setup and motivating partial order
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Weinstein domains via handle attachment and Weinstein homotopy, reviews exotic formally equivalent structures, and formulates classification questions together with the Weinstein cobordism relation and its contravariance for symplectic homology.

proof_stages.label: flexible Weinstein h-principle
proof_stages.summary: Loose Legendrian attaching spheres define flexible Weinstein domains, and the Cieliebak-Eliashberg h-principle gives topological existence and formal uniqueness, explaining how adding zigzags kills symplectic invariants.

proof_stages.label: single prescribed-Lagrangian construction
proof_stages.summary: As a sample construction, an exotic cotangent bundle of a sphere containing a chosen exact Lagrangian is built from the cotangent bundle of that Lagrangian by adding flexible or subcritical handles while preserving computable symplectic homology.

proof_stages.label: maximal theorem and Lagrangian applications
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that any finite collection of Weinstein domains in a fixed formal class has a common Weinstein overdomain, yielding exotic cotangent bundles containing many prescribed Lagrangians or smoothly isotopic but non-Hamiltonian-isotopic Lagrangian spheres.

proof_stages.label: minimal flexible decomposition theorem
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the maximal theorem is reduced to a minimality statement: every high-dimensional Weinstein domain can be homotoped to its flexible representative plus a smoothly trivial two-handle Weinstein cobordism carrying the symplectic information.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian handle-slide proof of minimality
proof_stages.summary: The minimal decomposition is proved by using Casals-Murphy Legendrian handle slides to make all but one top-index attaching Legendrian loose, while the remaining handle records the non-flexible symplectic data.

proof_stages.label: construction of the common maximal domain
proof_stages.summary: After replacing each given domain by the common flexible core plus its residual handles, the residual Legendrians are made disjoint and all handles are attached simultaneously to produce a nonunique Weinstein domain containing every input domain.

proof_stages.label: scope, nonuniqueness, and contact analogue
proof_stages.summary: The speaker emphasizes that the argument relies on Weinstein handle decompositions rather than arbitrary Liouville cobordisms and notes that analogous flexible/minimal/maximal constructions exist in contact topology.

examples.name: standard versus flexible cotangent bundle of a sphere
examples.note: The two Weinstein presentations of a cotangent bundle of a sphere, differing by a stabilized/loose attaching Legendrian, make concrete the distinction between formal symplectic equivalence and genuine symplectomorphism.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker gives the concrete Weinstein handle presentations for the standard and flexible cotangent bundles of the sphere and compares their attaching Legendrians and symplectic homology behavior.

passes.reason: The loose SH-zero flexible representative is minimal in its formal class, and the pair refutes formal/smooth uniqueness.

the standard cotangent bundle is the regular symmetric model while the flexible version is loose with vanishing symplectic homology, and the pair is used to refute formal/smooth classification up to symplectomorphism
The standard T^*S^n inherits the sphere’s high symmetry while the flexible version is loose/h-principle with vanishing SH, and the pair shows formal data do not determine the symplectic domain.
The canonical T^*S^n is highly regular while the flexible version is loose/minimal with vanishing SH, and the pair refutes formal/smooth uniqueness.
The highly symmetric standard T^*S^n is contrasted with its loose flexible minimal/SH-vanishing version to refute classification by formal smooth data.
the flexible stabilized cotangent-sphere structure has collapsed symplectic invariants, and the pair is used to show formal/smooth equivalence need not imply symplectomorphism
The standard and flexible cotangent bundles are formally the same but not symplectomorphic, with the flexible version loose/SH-vanishing and used to refute classification by formal smooth data.

The standard and loose/flexible cotangent structures are formally the same but distinguished by symplectic homology, refuting formal/smooth classification; the flexible member has deliberately loosened, SH-vanishing symplectic structure.
Used to show formal/smooth equivalence does not imply symplectomorphism; the standard sphere cotangent bundle is highly regular while the flexible version has collapsed SH-zero symplectic data.
The formally equivalent but non-symplectomorphic standard/flexible cotangent bundles refute formal classification, with the sphere model symmetric and the flexible member loose, SH-zero, and minimal.
They are used to show formal/smooth equivalence need not imply symplectomorphism; standard T^*S^n is highly symmetric and the flexible version is the loose, SH-zero minimal representative.
It shows formal/smooth data do not determine symplectic type, with the flexible member the vanishing-SH minimal h-principle representative.

examples.name: McLean's infinitely many exotic Weinstein domains
examples.note: McLean's formally equivalent but pairwise non-symplectomorphic domains are used to generate the classification, Lagrangian, symplectic-homology, and cobordism-relation questions that drive the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes McLean’s domains via a schematic handle presentation and then applies stabilization/flexibility to them to conclude they become Weinstein homotopic to the standard ball.

passes.reason: McLean’s infinite non-symplectomorphic family in one formal class refutes formal uniqueness without being sporadic.
McLean's domains form a complicated infinite exotic family, used to show that formally symplectomorphic Weinstein domains need not be symplectomorphic
McLean’s domains form an infinite exotic family, not a sporadic exception, and they refute classification by the common formal Weinstein type.
McLean's domains form an infinite non-sporadic exotic family used to show that one formal Weinstein class has many non-symplectomorphic members.
McLean's examples form a systematic infinite exotic family with rich SH^+ invariants, used to disprove formal/smooth classification.
McLean’s domains form an infinite exotic family, not a sporadic exception, and are cited to refute uniqueness within a formal Weinstein class

McLean's infinite exotic Weinstein domains are used to show formal symplectomorphism does not determine the symplectic domain, but they form a systematic infinite family rather than a sporadic exception.
McLean’s infinite family is used to show many formally symplectomorphic Weinstein domains are pairwise nonsymplectomorphic, but it is an infinite exotic family rather than a sporadic exception.
McLean's family refutes uniqueness/classification by formal symplectic type, but it is a systematic infinite family rather than a sporadic exception.
McLean’s infinite family is used to show formal symplectomorphism does not determine the Weinstein domain, but it is systematic rather than sporadic or extremal.
McLean’s infinite exotic family is used to show uniqueness/classification by formal symplectic data fails, but it is an infinite non-sporadic family with no stated extremal or symmetric structure.
McLean’s infinite family refutes classification by formal symplectic type, but is a systematic exotic family rather than sporadic or extremal.

examples.name: product ring analogy for the maximal domain
examples.note: The product of finitely many unital rings illustrates algebraically why a single object mapping to all the symplectic homologies is plausible, while the theorem supplies the geometric counterpart.
examples.engagement_reason: The product of unital rings is invoked only as an analogy for the maximal Weinstein domain construction.

passes.reason: The product ring is used as a universal/maximal object mapping to all factors, only as an analogy.
the product of rings is an ordinary systematic construction used only as an analogy for maps from the maximal Weinstein domain
The product ring is the universal/categorical product with projection maps to all factors, used only as an analogy for the maximal domain.
The product ring is invoked as the universal upper-bound analogue mapping to all factors, not as a refutation.
The product ring is a universal terminal cone mapping to all factors, invoked only as an analogy for the upper-bound construction.
the product of unital rings is used only as a standard universal analogy for maps to all factors, not to disprove a claim
The product ring is invoked only as an analogy, and structurally it is the universal product object with projection maps to all the given rings.
The product ring is used only as an analogy for a common upper/maximal construction, and structurally it is the categorical product/universal cone for maps to the given rings.
The product ring is only used as a standard categorical analogy for maps to all factors, not to disprove anything.
The product ring is a universal upper-bound analogue with maps to each factor, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
The product ring is only an analogy for the maximal-domain construction and is structurally universal as the categorical product with maps to all factors.
The product ring is used as a categorical-product analogue of a common maximal object mapping to all factors.

examples.name: ordinary smooth handle slide
examples.note: The familiar smooth handle slide is drawn first to orient the audience before the Legendrian handle-slide move used in the Weinstein proof is described.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws/describes the ordinary smooth handle slide step by step as one handle engulfing another before passing to the Legendrian analogue.

passes.reason: An ordinary smooth handle slide is a standard handlebody move used to motivate the Legendrian analogue.
an ordinary smooth handle slide is a standard handlebody move used illustratively to explain the Legendrian/Weinstein handle-slide argument
An ordinary smooth handle slide is a generic handle-calculus move used as a model for the Legendrian slide, not as an obstruction.
An ordinary smooth handle slide is a standard handlebody move used as a model for the Weinstein/Legendrian slide.
A smooth handle slide is the standard local handlebody move used illustratively to motivate the Legendrian analogue.
